column2: Introduction to the AI Video Workflow
column3: Students understand the flow: idea → script → visuals → voice → editing
column4: The teacher shows a short AI video and asks: “What steps do you think were used to make this video?”
column5: The teacher breaks down one video into its idea, script, visuals, voice, music, text, and editing.
column6: Students and the teacher build a production flow from a simple example: “A video about fun facts about cats.”
column7: Students create their own AI video workflow map in a notebook or worksheet.
column8: Students write down one new thing they learned and create a personal project folder.

column4: The teacher sparks ideas using safe themes: animals, the environment, school, light science, and positive habits.
column5: The teacher shows how to turn a broad topic into a short video idea. Example: “waste” → “why we should sort our waste”.
column6: The class creates three ideas from one sample theme, then selects the easiest idea to produce.
column7: Students write three personal video ideas, then select one main idea.
column8: Students save their chosen idea and write why they selected it.

column3: Title, main message, character, setting, duration
column4: The teacher reminds students that a short video should communicate one clear main message.
column5: The teacher demonstrates a video concept that includes a title, purpose, character, setting, mood, and duration.
column6: The class completes a concept template for the sample story “The Adventure of a Plastic Bottle”.
column7: Students develop their own concept: title, main message, character, setting, and a duration of 20–45 seconds.
column8: Students check whether their concept is simple enough to produce in 3–5 scenes.

column4: The teacher explains that the script contains the lines that will be read aloud or used as the voice-over.
column5: The teacher turns a concept into a script using this structure: opening, 3–4 content sentences, and closing.
column6: The class writes a five-sentence script from the sample concept.
column7: Students write a five-to-seven-sentence script for their own video.
column8: Students read their script slowly and mark any part that feels too long or difficult.

column4: The teacher explains that a storyboard is a plan for the scenes before the visuals are created.
column5: The teacher demonstrates how to divide a script into four scenes.
column6: The class breaks the sample script into an opening, problem, solution, and closing scene.
column7: Students divide their script into 3–5 scenes, describing the image and narration for each scene.
column8: Students save the storyboard and mark the most important scene.

column4: The teacher introduces the visual prompt formula: subject + place + action + mood + style.
column5: The teacher compares a weak prompt with a strong prompt for the opening scene.
column6: The class improves a sample prompt so that it is clearer and age-appropriate for elementary students.
column7: Students write visual prompts for scenes one and two of their own storyboard.
column8: Students check whether each prompt includes a character, place, action, mood, and style.

column4: The teacher reviews the prompts for scenes one and two and emphasizes consistent characters and style.
column5: The teacher shows how to write follow-up prompts that keep a character looking similar across several scenes.
column6: The class creates prompts for scenes three and four of the same sample video.
column7: Students complete prompts for every scene, with a minimum of three and a maximum of five scenes.
column8: Students mark any prompts that are still unclear and need revision.

column4: The teacher reviews safety rules: do not use a classmate’s face, personal data, or a real public figure without permission.
column5: The teacher generates an image from one prompt and demonstrates how to select the best result.
column6: The class compares two or three visual results and decides which one best matches the storyboard.
column7: Students generate visuals for every scene and select one best visual per scene.
column8: Students save the visuals with clear filenames: scene-1, scene-2, scene-3, and so on.

column4: The teacher shows examples of inconsistent visuals and asks what should be improved.
column5: The teacher demonstrates prompt revision by adding details about the character, color, background, or style.
column6: The class improves one prompt whose result does not yet match the concept.
column7: Students revise any visual that does not fit, then select the final version for each scene.
column8: Students make a list of final visuals and write one reason for choosing each visual.

column4: The teacher explains two voice-over options: recording the student’s own voice or using supervised AI voice or text-to-speech.
column5: The teacher reads the script with clear intonation and compares speech that is too fast with an appropriate pace.
column6: The class practices reading two sentences clearly, without rushing, and with pauses.
column7: Students record a voice-over or create one from their own script.
column8: Students save the audio file and check that it is clear, not too fast, and not too quiet.

column3: Scenes imported into the video editor
column4: The teacher explains that basic editing means arranging scenes in the order planned in the storyboard.
column5: The teacher opens Canva or CapCut, imports the visuals, arranges the scenes, and adds the voice-over.
column6: The class arranges two sample scenes in the editor.
column7: Students import every visual, put the scenes in order, and add the voice-over.
column8: Students save the first video draft and confirm that every scene is included.

column3: The video begins to feel alive
column4: The teacher explains that movement and transitions should stay simple so the video does not feel excessive.
column5: The teacher demonstrates a gentle zoom or pan and a simple transition between scenes.
column6: The class selects a transition that fits the sample video.
column7: Students add gentle movement to the visuals and transitions between scenes.
column8: Students replay the draft and note any section that still feels too fast or too slow.

column4: The teacher explains the purpose of music, sound effects, titles, and short captions.
column5: The teacher adds quiet music, adjusts the volume, and adds a title and key text.
column6: The class checks whether the narration is still clearer than the background music.
column7: Students add music, appropriate sound effects, a title, and short text to their own videos.
column8: Students save the near-final draft and check its audio, text, and duration.

column3: Revisions to content, audio, text, and safety
column4: The teacher shares a checklist: safe, clear, no misinformation, no mocking, and no personal data.
column5: The teacher assesses one sample video using the quality and ethics checklist.
column6: The class gives feedback on the sample video and identifies what needs to be improved.
column7: Students review their own video with the checklist and revise any weak areas.
column8: Students save the revised video and write down two improvements they made.

column4: The teacher explains how to export the video and deliver a short presentation.
column5: The teacher demonstrates video export and a 30-second presentation covering the title, idea, process, and most difficult part.
column6: The class creates a simple four-sentence presentation format.
column7: Students export the final video and practice a short presentation independently or with a partner.
column8: Students save the final file and write the opening sentence of their presentation.

column4: The teacher explains showcase rules: watch respectfully, show appreciation, and keep comments constructive.
column5: The teacher models short feedback: “I like…, and my suggestion is…”.
column6: The class practices giving feedback on one sample video.
column7: Every student presents a video, or a selection of students presents if time is limited; classmates give brief feedback.
column8: Students write a final reflection: what they learned, their favorite part, and what they want to try next.
